content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

This log records all verses in 2 Peter Chapter 2 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 2:1 — "secretly bring in" vs. "bring in alongside"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who will bring in alongside destructive heresies" | Literal rendering of pareisago -- the prefix para- ("alongside") is preserved to show the stealth of the introduction |
| LOGOS | "who will secretly bring in destructive heresies" | Formal rendering that captures the deceptive, covert connotation of the compound verb |
| PNEUMA | "They will cleverly introduce destructive heresies" | Dynamic rendering emphasizing the craftiness; adds "cleverly" as interpretive gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "secretly bring in" following LOGOS. The compound verb pareisago carries the clear sense of smuggling something in alongside something else. "Secretly" captures this better than EMET's overly literal "alongside" (which could confuse), while avoiding PNEUMA's added word "cleverly" which goes beyond the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:2 — "shameless ways" vs. "licentious ways" for aselgeiais

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the shameless deeds of them" | Renders aselgeiais as "shameless deeds"; preserves genitive construction |
| LOGOS | "their licentious ways" | Uses the more formal English equivalent "licentious" |
| PNEUMA | "their shameful ways" | Dynamic simplification; "shameful" is broader than the Greek term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "shameless ways" as a compromise. "Shameless" better reflects the brazen, unrestrained character of aselgeia (which implies a lack of restraint or shame) than "licentious" (too formal for general readership) or "shameful" (which reverses the sense -- the point is that they lack shame, not that they feel it). Changed "deeds" to "ways" for smoother English.

**Status:** Resolved -- composite of EMET's adjective and LOGOS's noun.

---

## 2:4 — "Tartarus" vs. "dark pits of the underworld"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having cast them into Tartaros" | Transliterates the Greek proper noun; preserves the mythological reference |
| LOGOS | "cast them into Tartarus" | Anglicized transliteration of the same Greek term |
| PNEUMA | "threw them into the dark pits of the underworld" | Dynamic interpretation that avoids the unfamiliar Greek name; combines the tartarosas verb with the "darkness" imagery |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Tartarus" following LOGOS. This is a hapax legomenon in the entire New Testament, and the author chose this specific mythological term deliberately. Replacing it with "dark pits of the underworld" (PNEUMA) loses the distinctive vocabulary and the intertextual resonance with Jewish apocalyptic literature (1 Enoch). A footnote in the Translator's Note addresses the term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:4 — "chains of darkness" vs. "dark pits" for seirais zophou

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "chains of darkness" | Follows Sinaiticus reading seirais zophou directly |
| LOGOS | "chains of darkness" | Same reading, same rendering |
| PNEUMA | "dark pits" | Conflates the variant reading sirois ("pits") with the Sinaiticus reading, producing a hybrid |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "chains of darkness" following EMET/LOGOS and the Sinaiticus reading (CΕΙΡΑΙC ΖΟΦΟΥ). PNEUMA's "dark pits" follows a variant not found in Sinaiticus.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Sinaiticus reading determinative.

---

## 2:5 — "the eighth person, Noah" phrasing

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the eighth one, Noah, a herald of righteousness, he guarded" | Hyper-literal; "the eighth one" placed first as in Greek word order; uses "guarded" for ephylaxen |
| LOGOS | "preserved Noah, the eighth person, a herald of righteousness" | Formal English word order; "preserved" for ephylaxen |
| PNEUMA | "he protected Noah, a preacher of righteousness, along with seven others" | Interprets "eighth" as "plus seven others"; replaces "herald" with "preacher" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The phrase "the eighth person" preserves the Greek idiom (ogdoon) while remaining intelligible. PNEUMA's "along with seven others" is a valid interpretation but loses the distinctive biblical idiom. "Preserved" is more natural English than EMET's "guarded" for ephylaxen. "Herald" is retained over PNEUMA's "preacher" as more accurate to keryx (a public crier/announcer).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:6 — Rendering of the condemnation clause

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having reduced to ashes, he condemned with an overthrow, having set them as an example to those about to be ungodly" | Preserves all participial structures and the noun katastrophe ("overthrow") |
| LOGOS | "condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to destruction, reducing them to ashes, having made them an example of what is coming upon the ungodly" | Restructures for clarity; uses "destruction" for katastrophe |
| PNEUMA | "reduced the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to ashes, condemning them to total destruction. He made them an example of what is going to happen to the ungodly" | Two sentences; adds "total" as intensifier |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a rendering close to LOGOS: "condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to ashes, having made them an example of what is coming upon the ungodly." The participial chain is restructured for English clarity while preserving the theological content. EMET's "overthrow" (katastrophe) is more literal but "to ashes" already conveys the destruction. PNEUMA's added "total" is not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with minor adjustment.

---

## 2:7 — "distressed" vs. "oppressed" for kataponoumenon

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "being oppressed by the conduct of the lawless ones" | "Oppressed" emphasizes external pressure; follows kata- prefix force |
| LOGOS | "who was distressed by the conduct of the lawless" | "Distressed" emphasizes internal suffering |
| PNEUMA | "who was distressed by the shameless immorality of the wicked people around him" | Expands with interpretive additions |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "distressed" following LOGOS. The verb kataponeomai can mean both "oppress" and "wear down/distress." In context, the emphasis is on Lot's internal torment (confirmed by v.8's reference to his soul being tormented), making "distressed" more fitting than "oppressed."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:8 — Structure of the "sight and hearing" clause

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for by sight and by hearing, the righteous one dwelling among them, day after day, his righteous soul was being tormented by their lawless works" | Preserves dative instrumentals "by sight and by hearing" at the front |
| LOGOS | "for by what he saw and heard, that righteous man, dwelling among them day after day, was tormented in his righteous soul by their lawless deeds" | Restructures the datives into a relative clause |
| PNEUMA | "that righteous man, living among them day after day, was tormented in his soul by the lawless deeds he saw and heard" | Moves the sensory reference to the end |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a rendering following LOGOS's structure but incorporating elements from all three: "for that righteous man, dwelling among them day after day, was tormented in his righteous soul by what he saw and heard of their lawless deeds." The sensory element is kept but repositioned for natural English flow.

**Status:** Resolved -- composite rendering.

---

## 2:10 — "lordship/authority" and "glories/glorious ones" for kyriotetos and doxas

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "despising lordship... blaspheming glories" | Ultra-literal: "lordship" for kyriotetos, "glories" for doxas |
| LOGOS | "despise authority... blaspheme the glorious ones" | "Authority" is more accessible; "glorious ones" interprets doxas as personal beings |
| PNEUMA | "despise authority... show no respect for the glorious ones" | Same interpretation; softens "blaspheme" to "show no respect" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Authority" communicates kyriotetos more clearly than "lordship." "The glorious ones" correctly interprets doxas as angelic beings (confirmed by v.11's comparison with angels). PNEUMA's "show no respect" loses the force of blasphemeo, which implies verbal attack, not mere disrespect.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:13 — "suffering wrong" vs. "being wronged" for adikoumenoi

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "being wronged as wages of unrighteousness" | Follows the distinctive Sinaiticus reading adikoumenoi; preserves the wordplay with adikia |
| LOGOS | "suffering wrong as the wages of wrongdoing" | Same Sinaiticus reading; renders the wordplay as "wrong/wrongdoing" |
| PNEUMA | "They will be paid back for the harm they have done" | Interprets dynamically; loses the Sinaiticus-specific reading |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "suffering wrong as the wages of wrongdoing." This preserves the distinctive Sinaiticus reading (adikoumenoi rather than the variant komizoumenoi, "receiving") and captures the wordplay between adikeo and adikia. PNEUMA's rendering, while clear, loses the manuscript-specific vocabulary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:13 — "stains" vs. "spots" for spiloi

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "spots and blemishes" | Literal rendering of spiloi kai momoi |
| LOGOS | "stains and blemishes" | "Stains" slightly more evocative in English |
| PNEUMA | "blots and blemishes" | "Blots" as dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "stains and blemishes" following LOGOS. All three renderings are defensible; "stains" carries the sense of permanent discoloration better than "spots" (which can sound trivial) or "blots" (which suggests ink marks).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor lexical preference.

---

## 2:14 — "adulteress" vs. "adultery" for moichalidos

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "eyes full of an adulteress" | Literal: moichalidos is genitive feminine singular, "of an adulteress" |
| LOGOS | "eyes full of adultery" | Renders the abstract sense; the idiom means they see every woman as an object of lust |
| PNEUMA | "Their eyes are full of adultery" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "adultery" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Greek moichalidos is literally "of an adulteress," but the expression is idiomatic -- it means their eyes are constantly seeking adultery, not fixated on a single woman. "Full of adultery" communicates the intended sense.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:14 — "accursed children" vs. "children of a curse"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "children of a curse" | Literal Semitic genitive: tekna kataras |
| LOGOS | "accursed children" | Converts the Semitic idiom to natural English |
| PNEUMA | "What a cursed brood!" | Dynamic exclamatory rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "accursed children" following LOGOS. This converts the Hebraic genitive construction into idiomatic English while retaining the gravity. PNEUMA's "cursed brood" is vivid but too colloquial. EMET's "children of a curse" preserves the Semitism but sounds unnatural.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:16 — "voiceless beast of burden" vs. "speechless donkey"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a voiceless beast of burden" | Literal: aphonon hypozygion; hypozygion means "animal under a yoke" |
| LOGOS | "a speechless donkey" | Identifies the animal specifically; "speechless" for aphonon |
| PNEUMA | "a donkey -- a speechless animal" | Appositional structure for clarity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a speechless donkey" following LOGOS. The referent is clearly Balaam's donkey (Numbers 22), and identifying the animal makes the allusion immediately recognizable. "Speechless" better captures aphonon (lacking voice/speech) than "voiceless." EMET's "beast of burden" is accurate for hypozygion but obscures the specific biblical reference.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:17 — "reserved" vs. "kept" for tetaretai

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "has been kept" | Literal rendering of tereo in perfect passive |
| LOGOS | "has been reserved" | "Reserved" implies intentional setting aside for a specific purpose |
| PNEUMA | "has been reserved" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "has been reserved" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Reserved" better conveys the deliberate, purposeful holding of darkness for these false teachers, compared to the more neutral "kept."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:18 — "swollen words" vs. "arrogant words" for hyperonka

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "speaking swollen words of vanity" | Literal: hyperonka means "over-swollen, puffed up" |
| LOGOS | "speaking arrogant words of vanity" | "Arrogant" captures the connotation more accessibly |
| PNEUMA | "They make pompous speeches that are empty and meaningless" | Full dynamic expansion; adds "empty and meaningless" for mataiotes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "arrogant words of vanity" following LOGOS. "Arrogant" captures the sense of hyperonka (literally "greatly swollen") more naturally than EMET's "swollen." PNEUMA's "pompous speeches that are empty and meaningless" is accurate in sense but expands well beyond the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:18 — "shameless deeds" vs. "licentiousness" for aselgeiais

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "by shameless deeds" | Consistent with v.2 rendering |
| LOGOS | "by licentiousness" | Formal register |
| PNEUMA | "By appealing to the lustful desires of the flesh" | Dynamic expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "by shameless deeds" for consistency with v.2 rendering, where the same word aselgeiais appears. This maintains a unified translation of the same Greek term throughout the chapter.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET for consistency.

---

## 2:19 — Gnomic statement on slavery

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for by what a person has been overcome, by this he has been enslaved" | Preserves the perfect tense indicating completed state |
| LOGOS | "for by whatever a person is overcome, by this he is enslaved" | Present tense as general principle |
| PNEUMA | "for you are a slave to whatever controls you" | Shifts to second person direct address; "controls" for hettaomai |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with present tense. The statement functions as a gnomic maxim (general truth), and English present tense serves this function better than EMET's perfect tense. PNEUMA's shift to second person ("you are a slave") is not in the Greek, which uses the impersonal third person.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:20 — "full knowledge" vs. "knowledge" for epignosei

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the full knowledge of the Lord and Savior Yeshua Christos" | Distinguishes epignosis from gnosis with "full knowledge"; retains Hebrew/Greek name forms |
| LOGOS | "the knowledge of the Lord and Savior Jesus Christ" | Standard "knowledge"; anglicized names |
| PNEUMA | "knowing our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ" | Verbal form; adds "our" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the knowledge" following LOGOS. While EMET's distinction between epignosis and gnosis is linguistically valid, English "full knowledge" is awkward and the prefix epi- is better handled in a footnote than in the text. PNEUMA's addition of "our" is not in the Greek. The name forms follow standard anglicization (Jesus Christ).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's note on epignosis preserved in Translator's Note.

---

## 2:20 — "Yeshua Christos" vs. "Jesus Christ" — name forms

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Yeshua Christos" | Preserves Hebrew/Greek forms throughout |
| LOGOS | "Jesus Christ" | Standard anglicized forms |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus Christ" | Standard anglicized forms |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Jesus Christ" following LOGOS/PNEUMA. Standard anglicized forms are used throughout the SOPHIA consensus text for accessibility.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Standing policy.

---

## 2:22 — Proverb rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "'A dog having returned to its own vomit,' and, 'A sow having been washed, to her rolling in the mud'" | Preserves participial structure; "rolling" for kylismon |
| LOGOS | "'A dog returns to its own vomit,' and, 'A sow, having washed, returns to wallowing in the mire'" | Supplies the verb "returns" for clarity; uses "mire" for borboros |
| PNEUMA | "'A dog returns to its own vomit,' and, 'A washed sow goes back to wallowing in the mud'" | Most natural English; "goes back" for the implied return |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Returns" is correctly supplied (implied by the context and the Proverbs 26:11 source). "Mire" preserves the literary register appropriate to a proverbial saying. "Wallowing" better captures kylismon (the act of rolling in filth) than EMET's plain "rolling."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Resolutions

| Verse | Key Issue | Resolution | Favored |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2:1 | "secretly bring in" vs. "bring in alongside" | "secretly bring in" | LOGOS |
| 2:2 | "shameless" vs. "licentious" vs. "shameful" | "shameless ways" | EMET/LOGOS composite |
| 2:4 | Tartarus vs. "dark pits of underworld" | "Tartarus" | EMET/LOGOS |
| 2:4 | "chains" vs. "pits" of darkness | "chains of darkness" | EMET/LOGOS (Sinaiticus) |
| 2:5 | "eighth person" phrasing | "Noah, the eighth person" | LOGOS |
| 2:6 | Condemnation clause structure | Restructured for clarity | LOGOS |
| 2:7 | "oppressed" vs. "distressed" | "distressed" | LOGOS/PNEUMA |
| 2:8 | Sight/hearing clause structure | Composite rendering | Composite |
| 2:10 | "lordship/glories" vs. "authority/glorious ones" | "authority" / "glorious ones" | LOGOS |
| 2:13 | adikoumenoi rendering | "suffering wrong as wages of wrongdoing" | LOGOS |
| 2:13 | "spots" vs. "stains" vs. "blots" | "stains" | LOGOS |
| 2:14 | "adulteress" vs. "adultery" | "adultery" | LOGOS/PNEUMA |
| 2:14 | "children of a curse" vs. "accursed children" | "accursed children" | LOGOS |
| 2:16 | "beast of burden" vs. "donkey" | "speechless donkey" | LOGOS |
| 2:17 | "kept" vs. "reserved" | "reserved" | LOGOS/PNEUMA |
| 2:18 | "swollen" vs. "arrogant" words | "arrogant" | LOGOS |
| 2:18 | aselgeiais consistency | "shameless deeds" | EMET (consistency) |
| 2:19 | Gnomic statement person/tense | Third person present | LOGOS |
| 2:20 | epignosis rendering | "knowledge" (not "full knowledge") | LOGOS |
| 2:20 | Name forms | "Jesus Christ" | LOGOS/PNEUMA |
| 2:22 | Proverb structure | Supplied "returns"; "mire" | LOGOS |

**Overall pattern:** LOGOS was the primary basis for the consensus text in this chapter (favored in 16 of 21 disagreements). EMET contributed the "shameless" vocabulary (vv.2, 18) and confirmed Sinaiticus readings. PNEUMA's dynamic renderings were generally too expansive but served as a useful check on clarity.

-- SOPHIA, Arbiter
